target: data/wins.json WIN-058b — rewrite r=0.96 vs r=0.992 inconsistency argument to pivot to transparency/specification failure (ISS-965)

findings.r_0_992_location: Current dome home page V51.1 Exhibit A explicitly labels it: "Correlation (r): 0.992 (Raw L1A residuals)." The parenthetical "Raw L1A residuals" specifies this as a temporal cross-correlation of level-1 post-residual time series — NOT a slope-fit R^2.
findings.r_0_96_location: No occurrence of 0.96 found on the current dome home, predictions, or context pages (grep of full HTML). PRED-KAPPA-001 on the current dome predictions page is about the ECLIPSE correction factor (1.672 = κ to 3 decimal places), not the 2003 Halloween storm — it does NOT claim r = 0.96. Our prior attribution ("PRED-KAPPA-001 claims r = 0.96") appears stale or mis-sourced and cannot be reconciled with the current dome site as of V51.1.
findings.implication: The curmudgeon-c2 advocate defense — that r = 0.96 and r = 0.992 measure different statistical quantities — is partially vindicated: 0.992 IS labeled as a temporal correlation. Our review must therefore NOT treat 0.96 vs 0.992 as "mutually inconsistent for the same statistic on the same data." The stronger pivot (per curmudgeon recommendation 2b) is the transparency/specification failure: the labeled r = 0.992 is a raw temporal correlation during a G5 event — exactly what standard electromagnetic-artifact physics predicts, and nothing is computed by repository code.

replacement_fields.finding: The κ coupling constant is post-hoc fitted from one extreme event, not derived from dome geometry. The claimed r = 0.992 correlation during the October 2003 G5 storm is unremarkable — Monte Carlo shows shared electromagnetic forcing produces r > 0.96 at physically realistic SNR — and the dome itself labels that figure as a temporal correlation of raw L1A residuals, not a slope fit of a coupling law. Neither the r-value nor the 10.9/6.5 = 1.677 ratio is computed by any script in the dome repository.
replacement_fields.detail_evidence:
The κ constant is not derived from dome geometry. No equation in the dome model derives κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67; actual ratio 10.9/6.5 = 1.677) from the disc radius (20,015 km), firmament height (9,086 km), or any other structural parameter. The constant appears as a fitted ratio between two observed quantities — magnetic field change and gravity residual — during a single extreme event (the October 2003 G5 solar storm, Dst = −383 nT). A ratio extracted from one event is a measurement, not a prediction.

The published r-value is labeled, but not computed. The current dome home page (V51.1, audited 2026-04-14) presents the 2003 Halloween-storm figure in Exhibit A as "Correlation (r): 0.992 (Raw L1A residuals)." The parenthetical specifies the statistic: a temporal cross-correlation of level-1 post-residual time series, not a slope-fit R2 of any coupling law. A secondary r = 0.96 figure that earlier dome materials associated with this storm is not present on the current public dome pages for the 2003 event and may reflect a prior version; we do not rely on a dome internal inconsistency here. The weakness of the claim does not depend on which r-value is authoritative — because no repository code takes the INTERMAGNET BOU and SG MB/MC series, runs a correlation, and outputs the published number. Like the 95.2% accuracy figure (since replaced by a hardcoded 94.5%), the r-values exist only as static HTML text. Readers are asked to accept the statistic without the computation that would allow independent verification.

The correlation itself is expected from shared forcing. During a G5 extreme geomagnetic storm, all geophysical instruments are driven simultaneously by massive ionospheric and ground-induced currents. Monte Carlo simulation of two independent sensors responding to a shared storm-envelope driver with autocorrelated noise (τ = 30 s, N = 1,620 samples matching the Exhibit A time window) shows the expected correlation depends entirely on signal-to-noise ratio. During a G5 storm (Dst = −383 nT), SG signals reach 5–10 µGal against a ~0.1 µGal instrument noise floor, giving SNR ≥ 50. At physically realistic SNR = 10, the simulation produces mean r = 0.990 (90% CI: 0.985–0.994) — the dome's r = 0.992 falls squarely within the expected range; even at SNR = 5 (ten times below the physical estimate) mean r still exceeds 0.96 as a robustness floor. High correlation between magnetic and gravimetric signals during extreme storms is the standard physics expectation from shared electromagnetic forcing (extreme geomagnetic storms simultaneously affect magnetometers via field variation and superconducting gravimeters via ground-induced current artifacts), not evidence of a new coupling mechanism. It would be surprising if r were significantly BELOW 0.99 during a G5 event. The dome's labeling the statistic as a raw L1A temporal correlation is in fact consistent with — and predicted by — the electromagnetic-artifact account: that is exactly the statistic that runs high during storm forcing.

The full analysis of the dome's dielectric coupling claims, including the Exhibit A/B statistical debunking, is covered in Part 7 (External Falsification Tests). See also WIN-012 for the κ = 1.677 single-pole collapse problem.

Note on dome-site numbering: The prospective predictions section lists WIN-058 as "The 1.67 Bedrock Induction Ratio" while the main predictions list assigns WIN-058 to "θ = −lonE: Unified Angular Coordinate Rule" (see WIN-058). We track both under separate IDs to ensure complete coverage.
replacement_fields.tldr_evidence: The coupling constant κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67) is a post-hoc ratio from one extreme geomagnetic event, not a dome derivation. The claimed r = 0.992 correlation is unremarkable: Monte Carlo shows any two sensors sharing a G5 storm driver produce r > 0.96 at physically realistic SNR, and the dome itself labels this figure as a temporal correlation of raw L1A residuals — exactly what standard electromagnetic-artifact physics predicts during storms. No repository code actually computes either the r-value or the 10.9/6.5 ratio; both are static HTML.
replacement_fields.tldr_verdict: The coupling constant is a post-hoc ratio from one event, not a dome derivation. The correlation it points to is expected from shared electromagnetic forcing during a G5 storm, and the WIN number also collides with a completely different claim on the dome site.

Duplicate numbering-collision paragraph collapsed into a single closing note (the prior wins.json text contained the same 'Note on dome-site numbering' paragraph twice; retained once).

anticipated_objections.objection: By removing 'mutually inconsistent' you have weakened the review — we are conceding a point the dome never made explicit.
anticipated_objections.response: We have not conceded anything the dome can point to as a victory; we have moved the argument from a contestable claim about what two r-values measure to an uncontestable one about what the repository actually computes. The dome labels r = 0.992 as 'Raw L1A residuals' — a temporal correlation. That is a shared-forcing statistic, not a coupling-law slope fit. The old framing gave the dome defender a free counter ('they measure different statistics'); the new framing eats that counter by accepting the dome's own label and showing the statistic it named is exactly the one electromagnetic-artifact physics predicts should run high during a G5 storm.

anticipated_objections.objection: You're ignoring the r = 0.96 figure — maybe it is a slope-fit statistic on an earlier version that the dome will resurrect.
anticipated_objections.response: If the dome republishes r = 0.96 with an explicit statistical label (slope-fit R², rank correlation, etc.) and a computation path in its public repo, we will reassess. As of V51.1 the figure is not present on the home, predictions, or context pages for the 2003 event. We will not build a falsification argument on a number the dome does not currently publish.

anticipated_objections.objection: Shared-forcing Monte Carlo is not a falsification of κ itself — you are only showing high r is expected, which the dome agrees with.
anticipated_objections.response: Correct, and we make that clear. The Monte Carlo is not offered as falsification of the coupling constant; it is offered as the null-expectation baseline. The dome presents r = 0.992 as evidence that the data 'confirms' the 1.67 slope; we show the same r-value is produced by two independent sensors sharing only G5 storm forcing, at SNR levels physically expected for superconducting gravimeters during Dst = −383 nT. The correlation therefore discriminates nothing. Falsification of κ comes from (a) its undefined status in the dome's own null-eclipse framework (WIN-013/014, see WIN-012), and (b) the absence of any geometrical derivation.

kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies that geomagnetic storms produce correlated signals across different geophysical instruments, and quantifying the coupling via a ratio κ is not inherently unreasonable. The Exhibit A figure — r = 0.992 on raw L1A residuals during the October 2003 G5 storm — is a real, correctly-computed-in-principle statistic (even though the dome publishes it without code). The claim fails because (a) a genuine coupling constant must be universal across many independent events, not a ratio from one G5 storm; (b) the correlation is fully explained by shared electromagnetic forcing (Crossley et al. 2013 on SG electromagnetic artifacts, Hinderer et al. 2007); and (c) the dome's own eclipse-gravity null results (WIN-013/014) already make κ's denominator vanish in its own framework.
